words: The first part of this interview was with DickcBenge, the second with his wife, ELLA
MAY ARDEN BENGE, and her daughter, PEGGY SHOTT. The interview took place
at their home on Hatter Creek, near Princeton, Idaho, on April 27, 1976.
SAM SCHRAGER: -- how your parents raised you.
DICK BENGE: Raised?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, how they raised you.

speaker: DB
words: I was raised in Nebraska, on a farm.

speaker: SS
words: You told me that, but I was wondering about how they did. I mean,

speaker: SS
words: how-- whether they were strict with you, or lenient, or made you toe

speaker: SS
words: the line, or what.

speaker: DB
words: Oh ^hey were lenient, but, Ihad to work. Dad, he wasn't very healthy,

speaker: DB
words: he had— them days they called it consumption. Well, the old feller,

speaker: DB
words: you know, he seen an advertisement in the paper— lung Germine

speaker: SS

speaker: DB

words: to get it. So, he took that for six months. And the glass, everywhere

speaker: DB
words: Huh?

speaker: DB
words: long. Now, if you'll go to some mountain region, you might be good

speaker: DB

speaker: DB

speaker: DB

speaker: DB

words: MRS. BENGE: Oh, yeah. He always liked it. He thought he liked to farm, but he

speaker: SS
words: didn't. And we was milkin' cows and when he started farming, "I

speaker: SS
words: ain't got time to fool with them." And he turned 'em all out. I

speaker: SS
words: was working at the mill at the time, and he should have been able to

speaker: SS
words: make it good, but he didn't.

speaker: PS
words: In my opinion, it all boils down to the theory if you're working for

speaker: PS
words: yourself, you take care of all the little jobs, see that all the

speaker: PS
words: little jobs are done. The big jobs'll take care of themselves. When

speaker: PS
words: it« time it gets done. But the little jobs; fixing the fence,

speaker: PS
words: the little jobs,^you take care of them, the big ones'll take care of

speaker: PS
words: themselves. And someone that works out for wages or really don't

speaker: PS
words: see the little jobs, take care of the big jobs, and the little jobsit

speaker: PS
words: collapses the big jobs.

speaker: PS
words: MRS. BENGE: Dick was, he was a master logger. He was very, very, very good at

speaker: PS
words: his job. Excellent logger. And people always come— oh, in late

speaker: PS
words: years, when it's changed so much, he probably wouldn't be so invaluable— "Come and

speaker: PS
words: look over this timber for me, lay where you think

speaker: PS
words: I should put the roads." And do this and do that. And he was a very,

speaker: PS
words: very excellent farmer.

speaker: SS
words: So Dick really had a fine reputation. That doesn't surprise me at

speaker: SS
words: all. Because he certainly seems to know logging from one end to the

speaker: SS
words: other.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Yes, he's a good logger and he understands it, and he knows how to

speaker: SS
words: take advantage of it. He knows what logs is. He's just real good

speaker: SS
words: at it. That's just the truth.

speaker: SS
words: Would you tell me a little more about this land clearing that you

speaker: SS
words: would do together? It was quite a job and one that would never end.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: After we got this steam engine, it wasn't so bad. But, I'll tell

speaker: SS
words: ya, this hillside up here, I know, I worked at it all one spring.

speaker: SS
words: And, Like I say, I put on coveralls— cover, you know, completelyand

speaker: SS
words: then when I'd come home,AI'd turn 'em wrong side out and come in

speaker: SS
words: and put cold cream on my face. I couldn't wash my face, because there

speaker: SS
words: was so much black, you know, so much of that lye in there. But that

speaker: SS
words: was hard work. Pick up and pull it over, and pile it together, and

speaker: SS
words: chop it up. AI think one spring when it got— when I had to quit be

speaker: SS
words: cause it got too dry, I counted sixty-three stumps that I could stand

speaker: SS
words: on and they would come up to here. It was getting kinda hot one Sat

speaker: SS
words: urday and some other boys was over there on the creek$layin' and I

speaker: SS
words: had all the kids up there picking up sticks and the older girls was

speaker: SS
words: in the house doing— gettin' supper and doing the ironing and things

speaker: SS
words: that had to be done, and Johnny'd stop and he'd look over at the

speaker: SS
words: creek and Dick was up there with us because it happened to be on

speaker: SS
words: Saturday, "Come on, here, pick that up. You get busy." And Peggy

speaker: SS
words: never had to be told. She just plowed right into it. And he looked

speaker: SS
words: over there and he said, "Oh, dear,"— and there was a dog a layin*

speaker: SS
words: in the shade of a log, and he said, "I wish I was a dog!" (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: "I wish I was a dog! " But, he learned to drive the Cat when he was

speaker: SS
words: quite young, and he then went to work for other farmers. Two years

speaker: SS
words: he worked for Walsters. Hugo Walster, down here. And, made good

speaker: SS
words: money.

speaker: SS
words: Before you married Dick and came up here, where was it that you lived

speaker: SS
words: before?

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Well, I was born in Pomeroy, Washington. And then, my mother died

speaker: SS
words: when Iwas eighteen months old, and then my grandparents took me to

speaker: SS
words: Winchester (Idaho). And I was there til I was about seven. Then

speaker: SS
words: we moved to Lewiston. And I was in Lewiston til I was eighteen—

speaker: SS
words: seventeen. Then I was twenty-two when I married Dick.

speaker: SS
words: What did you think of this area when you first moved over here?

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Well, I come up and got a job as a hasher— working in a logging camp

speaker: SS
words: in the cookshack, you know. Waiting table . And, oh, I was just

speaker: SS
words: busy as a bee, you know. Just aflyin' around there. And this guy

speaker: SS
words: settin' over there, see. And he smiled at me so nice and said, "Is

speaker: SS
words: there any warm hotcakes?" And, I looked him over— and he was thirty

speaker: SS
words: and I thought, "Huh, boy, I'll bet he's good to his wife." Then I

speaker: SS
words: had a blind date, and it turned out to be him. And, I looked at him

speaker: SS
words: and I said, "Oh, so you're the one they call Dick Benge, are you?"

speaker: SS
words: He said, "Part of the time." And, my goodness, I couldn't get rid of

speaker: SS
words: him! I couldn't get rid of him, he was right there every time I

speaker: SS
words: turned around.

speaker: SS
words: He must have taken a liking to you.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: He evidently did, because, he was right there all the time. He was

speaker: SS
words: more lumberjack style. He didn't have anything the first two times he took me out

speaker: SS
words: except anew pair of bib overalls and aclean shirt.

speaker: SS
words: And one time he wore a pair of Malone pants. And the next time he

speaker: SS
words: come to get me he had a brand new suit, and his tie was on crooked!

speaker: SS
words: Bless his heart! Yep. His tie was on crooked.

speaker: SS
words: I was gonna ask you about church, too. Around here when you were

speaker: SS
words: first married and with the little kids, was there a community church

speaker: SS
words: here in Princeton at that time?

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Well, yes. But then they went mostly to the Nazarene Church. cause the

speaker: SS
words: Nazarene minister would come out and get them, and I didn't

speaker: SS
words: go. Wasn't that terrible?

speaker: SS
words: Not at all.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: I wish I would have. But I didn't. Then after I became a Christian

speaker: SS
words: I prayed so hard — my kids went to Bible School. They weren't com

speaker: SS
words: pletely ignorant. And they went to Sunday School,— that they would

speaker: SS
words: come to the Lord, they did. Peggy joined the Catholic

speaker: SS
words: Church, and Judy joined the Lutheran and Johnny joined the Baptist

speaker: SS
words: and (kitha joined the Christian. Johnny's a Mason now.

speaker: SS
words: P.S.: And we have one sister that is a Mormon, and one's a First Christian.

speaker: SS
words: Religion is very seldom discussed with a family, that way.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Cora Belle joined the Mormon Church.

speaker: SS
words: P.S.: Mormon, Catholic, Lutheran,. Christian, Mason.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Well, that should be a lesson for me, shouldn't it? Yeah. It doesn't

speaker: SS
words: matter where they come, as long as they belong to the Lord. We just

speaker: SS
words: don't discuss it too much. We don't avoid it. No.

speaker: SS
words: PS.: But no discussions about it.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like the family, though, is still kind of close.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Oh, you'll never find a family that's closer. You never could find

speaker: SS
words: a family that was any clos(t)er. Or that stick together when anything

speaker: SS
words: happens to one. They're right there.

speaker: SS
words: In fact, my sister is probably the best friend I've ever had. Judy

speaker: SS
words: the one that was here, when you were here before. We're just real

speaker: SS
words: good friends. I don't think I've ever had a better friend. Sister?

speaker: SS
words: It doesn't make abit of difference, she's my friend. We've always

speaker: SS
words: been real close. And my husband and her are especially close. Now

speaker: SS
words: this sounds ridiculous, because they're not related. Imean, but

speaker: SS
words: they're just exceptionally close. I don't think that if either one

speaker: SS
words: P.S.: of them ever needed help, I am sure that's just right where they'd

speaker: SS
words: go. And this is great! He's far closer to her than he is to any

speaker: SS
words: of his sisters and she is far closer to him than her brother. We're

speaker: SS
words: close to my brother, but family wise, we don't know him any more as

speaker: SS
words: a friend, let's put it that way; he's a brother, sure, we love him,

speaker: SS
words: but we don't know him as a person because he's been in California

speaker: SS
words: for fifteen years. And as a friend— we know he's there if we need

speaker: SS
words: him. But distance has lessened this friendship-type thing.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: When Judy was growing up, you know, and starting to go with the boys,

speaker: SS
words: she was a few years younger than Johnny, and Boy, he kept an eagle

speaker: SS
words: eye on her. I'm tellin' you. (Chuckles) One time in Princeton the

speaker: SS
words: lavoratories was out behind the house, and she come and asked me if

speaker: SS
words: she could go out, and I said, "Come right back." And I was helping

speaker: SS
words: back in the kitchen, you know, and I guess she didn't even get that

speaker: SS
words: far because there was some boys out there started a hollerin' at her,

speaker: SS
words: you know, and Johnny just went and grabbed her around the waist and

speaker: SS
words: brought her in the house and sit her down! She bawled a while, and I

speaker: SS
words: just thought, weii, let'em—Alet him handle it. And pretty soon I

speaker: SS
words: looked around and they was out on the floor dancin'. And if she was

speaker: SS
words: goin' with a new boy that he wasn't sure about, he'd follow 'em all

speaker: SS
words: the way home, and back his car up back of their's and wait til

speaker: SS
words: she come in the house. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Do you think families are as close now as the families used to be in

speaker: SS
words: the early days? Seems to me the families used to be a lot closer

speaker: SS
words: than the average is today.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: We're all close together when we're together, but the three of us

speaker: SS
words: are here, and then Clair's clear down in lower California and Johnny's

speaker: SS
words: up around Arcadia, and we don't see Clair so very much, she'd got

speaker: SS
words: six kids and she's got about all she can handle.

speaker: SS
words: P-S: I think the difference is that , we strive so much to become individuals,

speaker: SS
words: they st^Lve for this from the time they're in the first grade

speaker: SS
words: on, to become an individual; do your own thing, you know this type

speaker: SS
words: of thing. And families are close but when they get in school and

speaker: SS
words: each individual goes their own way. And we used to all go the same

speaker: SS
words: way. Another thing that's good or bad, but I think it has done a

speaker: SS
words: lot to destroy— not destroy— it doesn't encourage this family unit

speaker: SS
words: closeness. Of course, we were just very fortunate, and the boys are

speaker: SS
words: so very, very close.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: And they're all together, yet.

speaker: SS
words: P.S.: Yeah, they're all home at least once a week, and even when I'm down

speaker: SS
words: here, they all come at least once a week and sometimes twice a week.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that maybe the most of families were tended to be real

speaker: SS
words: close, like back in the early days when you were growing up? Do you

speaker: SS
words: think families were a lot closer then than most of 'em are nowdays?

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Well, see, I only had one half sister. And my grandparents raised me.

speaker: SS
words: That's right.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: And, of course, in the summer when school was out, we'd go around

speaker: SS
words: and visit all summer to each one of the boys, you know. And she had

speaker: SS
words: seven sons and a daughter. And, my father married again, and we'd

speaker: SS
words: go and visi t every one of 'em. Oh, stay a week, ten days and maybe

speaker: SS
words: with my Aunt than that. So, I got to know all of my cou

speaker: SS
words: sins real well. And I guess the family was close. They never had a

speaker: SS
words: great many family gatherings; there were nearly always some of 'em

speaker: SS
words: there. And, then, the ones that lived close would come for Christ

speaker: SS
words: mas dinner, but they never come very far to come to a big dinner. I

speaker: SS
words: try to have Thanksgiving dinner. I have pretty good luck, they pret

speaker: SS
words: ty much come, and you can't insist on— if it was your kids, but

speaker: SS
words: your grandkids kinda get tired of going up to the old folks. They

speaker: SS
words: don't act it, but then, they pretty much come, don't they Peggy?

speaker: SS
words: P.S.: Yes. Transportation in those days did alter the fact that they moved

speaker: SS
words: away. That they weren't as close then.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Johnny has been up here he came up in August, wasn't it?

speaker: SS
words: P.S.: He was here in January.

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: For a reunion? And he's been up once since.

speaker: SS
words: P.S.: I meant when you were growing up, do you think transportation did

speaker: SS
words: alter the ?

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: P.S.: The family togetherness type of thing?

speaker: SS
words: Did you have much to do with the school? Did parents become much

speaker: SS
words: involved in it?

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: No. The only thing is that I gave dances down here in the schoolhouse,

speaker: SS
words: About four of 'em, to get money to buy— when we had the log schoolhouse

speaker: SS
words: down here.

speaker: SS
words: To get money to buy what?

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Seats, and some more blackboards and things for the school. And then

speaker: SS
words: the only other thing I done was when they were consolidating and the

speaker: SS
words: board said we cannot afford a bus, we just can't. And Peggy was married

speaker: SS
words: then, weren't you?A And I put on a small half hour play, and

speaker: SS
words: two smaller ones. And then had a basket social and a dance. And

speaker: SS
words: then when we had that I got up and announced that if I could get

speaker: SS
words: enough volunteers that wanted to be in it, that we would put on a

speaker: SS
words: full length play, and maybe we could raise the rest of the money for

speaker: SS
words: the bus. We put it on three times. But I never worked with a group

speaker: SS
words: of children, or a group of young people that was so willing and

speaker: SS
words: worked together so good. And then, another thing, they were all high

speaker: SS
words: schoolers, and they would stop as they came home on the bus, most of

speaker: SS
words: 'em was on the bus— and Iwould be there and have the hall warm,

speaker: SS
words: and we'd practice until about six-thirty or seven. And then they

speaker: SS
words: took turns bringing food, you know, and we'd eat our dinner and wash

speaker: SS
words: our dishes. And then practice until nine. And that wasn't so late

speaker: SS
words: but what the kids could go home and do some homework if they had to.

speaker: SS
words: About three nights a week. And we put it on three times.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember what play it was?

speaker: SS
words: : 0h yes Object Matrimony. And they was twelve children in it. I

speaker: SS
words: don't know, the one down hereVas a two and ahalf hour play and we

speaker: SS
words: had something in between each play, you know, there was three acts,

speaker: SS
words: and it was all one scene, but—

speaker: SS
words: What was Object Matrimony about? Sounds like it was about marriage.

speaker: SS
words: Object Matrimony. Well, there were these girls, these six girls,

speaker: SS
words: and there was no boys around or anything, and they was wantin' boy

speaker: SS
words: friends, and they didn't know, so one of 'em— one of the boys come

speaker: SS
words: in, that was a reporter there and he said, "Well, let me put it in

speaker: SS
words: the paper." Idon't remember what the ad was, but then, he put it in

speaker: SS
words: the paper, and then these guys showed up. And Clair was the cook,

speaker: SS
words: and she was an older girl, and Johnny was the old soldier— dressed

speaker: SS
words: up in an old soldier suit— that come courtin' her! Everybody got

speaker: SS
words: married in the end. (Chuckles) All six of 'em! And it was cute,

speaker: SS
words: wasn't it honey?

speaker: SS
words: P.S.: Yeah, it was. Lot of work.

speaker: SS
words: And it{quite a bit of money then?

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: Yes, it raised the money.

speaker: SS
words: What hall was this? Was this in the schoolhouse?

speaker: SS
words: MRS. BENGE: It was inAPrinceton Hall.

speaker: SS
words: The Princeton Hall?

speaker: SS
words: Sounds really like fun.

speaker: SS
words: P.S.: That isn't the first time you had done it. Other high school kids

speaker: SS
words: wouldn't have anything to do in the winter, you know, and gas was

speaker: SS
words: at a premium; it wasn't available, during the war; we had stamps to

speaker: SS
words: get gas, and when the stamps were gone, that was it.

speaker: SS
words: END
